criteria: The agent has read Section 2.1 completely to understand the MEMAGENT workflow: processing text segment-by-segment, maintaining a fixed-length memory via an overwrite strategy, and decomposing inference into Context-Processing and Answer-Generation modules.

The agent has read Section 2.2 completely to understand the Multi-Conv DAPO training algorithm, including the extension of the loss computation from (group, token) to (group, conversation, token) dimensionality.

The agent has read Section 3.1 completely to understand the experimental setup: backbone models (Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct and Qwen2.5-14B-Instruct), the two-stage curriculum RL strategy, context window allocation (1024 query + 5000 chunk + 1024 memory + 1024 output = 8K), and all evaluation benchmarks.

The agent has read Appendix A.1 to understand the two prompt templates: (1) the context-processing template that takes problem, memory, and chunk as input and outputs updated memory, and (2) the answer-generation template that takes problem and memory and outputs a boxed answer.

The agent has read Appendix A.3 to understand all training hyperparameters: KL factor 1e-3, AdamW optimizer with lr=1e-6, linear warmup with 20 warmup steps, rollout batch size 256, group size 16, off-policy ratio 16, and no entropy loss.

The plan includes implementation of the MEMAGENT context-processing loop: iterating over document chunks, updating the memory after each chunk using the memory-update prompt template, and starting from an empty memory.
The plan includes implementation of the MEMAGENT answer-generation module: given the final accumulated memory and the original question, generating the boxed answer.

The plan includes the Multi-Conv DAPO training algorithm, treating each context-independent conversation as an independent optimization objective with advantage derived from the final conversation applied to all preceding conversations.
The plan includes the two-stage curriculum training strategy: Stage I with HotpotQA-based RULER synthetic data (~32K tokens each) and Stage II with DocQA-RL-1.6K mixed data (max 60K tokens).
The plan includes evaluation on all four benchmark categories: RULER-HQA, LongBench-QA, NIAH (three levels), and LongBench-SUM (GovReport and QMSum).

The context-processing loop is implemented: the model processes document chunks one at a time, and after reading each chunk, overwrites the previous memory with an updated one using the memory-update prompt template.
The answer-generation module is implemented: after all chunks are processed, the model consults only the problem statement and the final memory to produce its answer formatted with \boxed{}.

The prompt template for context-processing is implemented exactly as in Table 5 (top), containing placeholders for {prompt}, {memory}, and {chunk}, and prompting the model to update memory while retaining relevant details.
The prompt template for answer-generation is implemented exactly as in Table 5 (bottom), containing placeholders for {prompt} and {memory}, and instructing the model to put the answer in \boxed{}.
The 8K context window allocation is correctly implemented: 1024 tokens for query, 5000 tokens for context chunk, 1024 tokens for memory, and 1024 tokens for output.

The Multi-Conv DAPO advantage computation is implemented as Equation 1: the advantage for all conversations from the same sample is derived from the final-answer conversation reward minus the group mean reward, without division by standard deviation.

The Multi-Conv DAPO loss function is implemented as Equation 2: summing over (group, conversation, token) indices with clipped importance-sampling ratio and KL divergence penalty from the reference model.
The clipped importance-sampling ratio in the DAPO loss is correctly implemented using asymmetric clipping bounds (1 - epsilon_low, 1 + epsilon_high) as shown in Equation 2.
The rule-based reward function is implemented as in Equation 3: R(y_hat, y) = 1 if is_equiv(y, y_hat) else 0, comparing the predicted answer from the final conversation against the ground truth.

The multi-conversation rollout is implemented correctly: for each sample, G groups are generated where each group consists of multiple conversations (context-processing turns + one final answering turn), and the reward is derived only from the final answer conversation.
The Multi-Conv DAPO algorithm (Algorithm 1) is fully implemented using the verl framework, with off-policy training (ratio of sample batch size to backpropagation batch size set to 16).
The training uses the AdamW optimizer with a constant learning rate of 1e-6, linear warmup scheduler with 20 warmup steps, KL factor beta=1e-3, rollout batch size 256, and group size 16.

Stage I training data is prepared: 32,768 synthetic QA instances generated using the RULER methodology on HotpotQA, embedding golden paragraphs within extensive distractor content from the same dataset, each approximately 32K tokens in length.

The HotpotQA data filtering pipeline is implemented: filtering out questions where Qwen2.5-7B-Base or Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct achieves 100% Best-Of-2 score without context (common knowledge filtering), resulting in approximately 50% of data being retained.
Stage II training data is prepared: 2,560 instances with a maximum length of 60K tokens, composed of DocQA-RL-1.6K mixed with Stage I data.
The RULER-HQA test sets with different context lengths are synthesized: ranging from approximately 7K to 3.5M tokens, with 50 to 6,400 wiki items from HotpotQA.
The evaluation uses the sub-EM (substring exact match) metric: an answer is considered correct if the model's extracted answer contains all elements of the ground truth.
Answer normalization is applied before evaluation: removing definite articles, ignoring case distinctions, and applying other standard normalization steps.
Answers are extracted from model outputs using regular expressions, with the model prompted to respond in the format 'the answer is ANSWER' or \boxed{} format.

The NIAH benchmark evaluation is implemented using three levels from RULER (niah_single_1, niah_single_2, niah_single_3) with the specified configurations: level 1 (repeat haystack, word key, number value), level 2 (essay haystack, word key, number value), level 3 (essay haystack, word key, UUID value).
The LongBench-QA evaluation is implemented on five datasets: NarrativeQA, Qasper, HotpotQA, 2WikiMultihopQA, and MuSiQue.
The LongBench-SUM evaluation is implemented using ROUGE-{1,2,L} recall scores on GovReport and QMSum datasets.
The Stage I training is executed successfully for approximately 400 steps until convergence, using Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct and Qwen2.5-14B-Instruct as backbone models.
The Stage II training is executed successfully after Stage I convergence using the mixed DocQA-RL-1.6K and Stage I data.
RULER-HQA evaluation is executed successfully for RL-MEMAGENT-14B and RL-MEMAGENT-7B across context lengths from 7K to 3.5M tokens.
LongBench-QA evaluation is executed successfully for MEMAGENT-14B and MEMAGENT-7B across all five datasets (2Wiki, HQA, MuSiQue, NQA, Qasper).
NIAH evaluation is executed successfully for RL-MEMAGENT across context lengths up to 512K, covering all three difficulty levels.
LongBench-SUM evaluation is executed successfully for RL-MEMAGENT-14B and RL-MEMAGENT-7B on GovReport and QMSum.
The ablation study comparing RL-MEMAGENT vs. MEMAGENT without RL is executed on RULER-HQA and LongBench-QA.
The memory-length ablation study is executed with memory sizes ranging from 256 to 4096 tokens (keeping total context length within 8192) on both NIAH and LongBench-QA benchmarks.

The RULER-HQA results match Table 1: RL-MEMAGENT-14B achieves approximately 80%+ accuracy across all context lengths from 7K to 896K, and approximately 79% and 71% at 1.75M and 3.5M tokens respectively, demonstrating near-lossless extrapolation.
The RULER-HQA results match Table 1: RL-MEMAGENT-7B achieves approximately 81% accuracy at 7K and maintains above 71% even at 3.5M tokens, outperforming all baseline models by a large margin.

The LongBench-QA results match Table 3: MEMAGENT-14B achieves approximately 79.0 on 2Wiki, 73.0 on HQA, 52.0 on MuSiQue, 25.0 on NQA, 26.0 on Qasper (AVG ~51.0), outperforming larger models like QwenLong-L1-32B and DS-Distill-Qwen-32B.

The NIAH results match Figure 5: RL-MEMAGENT achieves over 95% accuracy on the NIAH benchmark at 512K context length, with a performance loss of less than 5%, while baseline models show substantial degradation.

The LongBench-SUM results match Table 2: RL-MEMAGENT-14B achieves SOTA (37.16 ROUGE-1, 12.03 ROUGE-2, 16.23 ROUGE-L, AVG 21.80) on GovReport, and strong performance on QMSum (50.21 ROUGE-1, AVG 31.39).

The RL ablation results match Figures 6-7: MEMAGENT without RL shows significant performance degradation as context length increases on RULER-HQA, and only marginal or negative improvements on LongBench-QA compared to the backbone model, while RL-MEMAGENT achieves significant improvements in both scenarios.

The memory-length ablation results match Tables 8-9 and Figures 8-9: the default configuration (m=1024, c=5000) achieves a reasonable performance sweet spot, and MEMAGENT performance is robust across different memory sizes (256-4096).

The context distribution probing experiment results match Table 4: RL-MEMAGENT remains consistently robust (within ±5% of random baseline) across all five context distribution patterns (0%-20%, 0%-100%, 20%-80%, 40%-60%, 80%-100%), demonstrating immunity to information-overwritten and lost-in-the-middle phenomena.

The RAG agent baseline comparison results match Tables 10-11: RL-MEMAGENT-14B outperforms RAG+Qwen2.5-14B with K=8 on RULER-HQA (80.47 vs 78.12 at 7K, and maintaining advantage at extreme lengths like 3.5M), and significantly outperforms RAG baselines on LongBench-QA (51.0 AVG vs 39.42 best RAG).
